primaryAngle: Belleville's storm recovery: tornado, hail, and ongoing insurance-claim work

dominantEras: early-1900s through mid-century brick bungalows and two-story frame

neighborhoods.name: Belleville Historic Districts
neighborhoods.profile: Three federally-registered historic districts in the downtown core, including West Main Street and Old Belleville, with 19th- and early-20th-century brick stock.

neighborhoods.name: West Belleville
neighborhoods.profile: Older established neighborhoods west of the historic core; mature trees and pre-WWII brick and frame homes.

neighborhoods.name: Northwest growth corridor
neighborhoods.profile: 1970s–2010s subdivisions on the city's northern and northwestern sides; mostly asphalt-shingle production housing.

neighborhoods.name: Swansea border / South Belleville
neighborhoods.profile: Mixed older and newer housing approaching the Swansea border; includes mid-century ranch and infill.

weatherEvents.description: Southern Illinois tornado risk window (March–June). Belleville and the broader Metro East have experienced multiple tornado events in recent decades (specific dated events: owner-verify).
Hail events frequently accompany Metro East severe weather, producing widespread insurance-claim activity in spring (specific dated events: owner-verify).
Derecho and straight-line wind events have caused tree-fall and roof damage in Belleville (specific events: owner-verify).

commonRoofingChallenges: High insurance-claim volume from recurrent tornado and hail activity
Historic-district approvals on downtown brick stock can require Historic Preservation Commission review

uniqueAngleElaboration: Belleville sits in southern Illinois's tornado and severe-weather corridor, and storm-recovery work is a defining part of roofing in the city. Spring tornado risk peaks April–June, and major hail events have repeatedly produced widespread insurance-claim activity across the Metro East. Belleville roofers are insurance-claim contractors first and material specialists second — the conversation almost always starts with what the storm did and what the carrier will cover. Layered on top of that is Belleville's significant historic stock: federally-registered districts in the downtown core, brick homes that often have built-in gutters and roofing details that don't apply to suburban asphalt work, and preservation-commission review for visible material changes on historically contributing properties. Roofers working here need active Illinois licensure, comfort meeting Illinois adjusters on-site, and the experience to spot tornado-driven structural damage that hides under apparently intact shingles.
sourcesConsulted: National Register of Historic Places listings for Belleville, IL
Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation roofing licensure
